verdict_reasoning: SRC is a cytoplasmic tyrosine kinase canonically resident in the cytosol and inner leaflet of the PM. However, it has a documented dual localization with a minority surface pool through dynamic trafficking and activation-induced recruitment to membrane microdomains (lipid rafts, focal adhesions). Additionally, SRC becomes prominently surface-exposed in oncogenic contexts — many cancer cells display elevated ecto-SRC with catalytic activity directed toward extracellular substrates and ligands. This cancer-cell ecto-kinase pool is a well-established phenomenon. Surface-directed therapeutic programs (monoclonal antibodies, kinase inhibitors targeting the PM-resident pool) exist. The PM pool is not merely transient equilibration but reflects active trafficking and signaling complexes; it qualifies as dual_localization / cell_state_induced contextual surface accessibility.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Baseline cytoplasmic localization is canonical; the surface pool is minority and activation/context-dependent. Uncertainty centers on whether the steady-state PM minority pool is large enough or stable enough to qualify independently, or whether SRC is primarily surface-accessible only when co-trafficked as part of a complex (e.g., receptor-tyrosine-kinase signaling platforms). Cancer-cell ecto-display is well-documented but not baseline. A case could also be made for cell_state_induced if cancer-state ecto-kinase is the dominant non-baseline surface story.
